elements: color-coded edges (green for orientation, red for depth)

interactions.teaches: Hovering over edges highlights them, helping you differentiate between orientation and depth discontinuities.
Clicking an edge explains how its type (orientation or depth) contributes to the 3D perception of the structure.

demo_steps.title: Inspecting 3D Edge Types
demo_steps.narration: Observe the cubes' orientation; green edges indicate orientation changes, while red edges denote depth changes. This helps us understand how various edges convey 3D information.

demo_steps.title: Depth Discontinuities Explained
demo_steps.narration: Notice the distinct lines representing depth discontinuities. These prominent edges are crucial for understanding the spatial arrangement and the separation between objects in our visual field.

demo_steps.title: Contact Edges and Surface Relationships
demo_steps.narration: Look at where the cubes meet — contact edges. These edges reveal how surfaces and objects relate to one another in space, enhancing our 3D perception.

plan.camera_suggestion: Frontal view replicating the original image with a slight tilt to highlight depth variations and edge orientations.

plan.contextChunk:
$$\begin{aligned}
{\bf n} = \frac{\nabla \ell}{\lVert \nabla \ell \rVert}
\end{aligned}$$


The first decision that we will perform is to decide which pixels
correspond to edges (regions of the image with sharp intensity
variations) and which ones belong to uniform regions (flat surfaces). We
will do this by simply thresholding the edge strength $e(x,y)$. In the
pixels with edges, we can also measure the edge orientation
$\theta(x,y)$. @fig-gradient and @fig-edge visualize the edges and the normal vector on each edge.

![Gradient and edge types.](figures/simplesystem/gradient.png){#fig-gradient}

![](figures/simplesystem/edgeTypes.png){#fig-edge}


## From Edges to Surfaces

We want to recover world coordinates $X(x,y)$, $Y(x,y)$, and $Z(x,y)$
for each image location $(x,y)$. Given the simple image formation model
described before, recovering the $X$ world coordinates is trivial as
they are directly observed: for each pixel with image coordinates
$(x,y)$, the corresponding world coordinate is $X(x,y) = x$. Recovering
$Y$ and $Z$ will be harder as we only observe a mixture of the two world
coordinates (one dimension is lost due to the projection from the 3D
world into the image plane). Here we have written the world coordinates
as functions of image location $(x,y)$ to make explicit that we want to
recover the 3D locations of the visible points.

elements: edge annotations for 3D orientation (green and red lines)

interactions.teaches: Hovering highlights the 3D orientation edges, showing how orientation affects edge visibility.
Clicking on depth discontinuities reveals how edges are formed at different depths in the scene.

demo_steps.title: Understanding 3D Orientation
demo_steps.narration: Here are three different 3D cubes with distinct orientations. Notice how the edges are colored green and red to indicate different sides and orientations.

demo_steps.title: Exploring Depth Discontinuities
demo_steps.narration: Depth discontinuities are displayed as thicker black lines. These lines represent changes in depth that contribute to the perception of 3D structure.

demo_steps.title: Identifying Contact Edges
demo_steps.narration: Contact edges are shown where the cubes meet. These are critical for understanding the relationship between surfaces and how objects interact in space.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Frontal view with slight tilt to emphasize depth and orientation changes.
